channel.title: Butlerian Biopolitics on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Butlerian Biopolitics on emsenn.net

item.title: Frames of War
item.description: Frames of war, as Butler develops the concept in Frames of War: When Is Life Grievable? (2009), names the visual, discursive, and institutional frames through which war is presented to publics — and through which the lives destroyed in war are rendered either grievable or invisible. The frame is not a neutral window onto events. It is a structure that determines what appears within it and what is excluded, what registers as a life and what does not, what counts as violence and what counts as security.

item.title: Precarious Life
item.description: Precarious life, as Butler develops it in Precarious Life: The Powers of Mourning and Violence (2004), names the condition of exposure to violence, deprivation, and abandonment that is not equally shared but differentially distributed. All human life is precarious in the ontological sense — every body is vulnerable to injury, illness, and death. But precariousness in the political sense is produced: specific populations are made precarious by the withdrawal of legal protection, economic security, and social recognition, while other populations are shielded by the same structures that expose the first.

item.title: Grievability
item.description: Grievability, as theorized by Judith Butler in Precarious Life (2004) and Frames of War (2009), names the condition under which a life is recognized as worth mourning — and therefore worth protecting. Grievability is not a response to death but a precondition for recognizing life as life in the first place. A life that is not apprehended as grievable is not fully apprehended as living. The question of who can be grieved is prior to the question of who can be killed.
